question:
5. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) Solve the system of differential equations
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{d x}{d t}=x+y-3 \\
\frac{d y}{d t}=-2 x+3 y+1
\end{array}
\]
subject to the conditions \( x=y=0 \) for \( t=0 \).
(page 101)
(ii) A heavy particle is attached to the end \( A \) of a light \( \operatorname{rod} A B \) of length \( a \). The rod is hinged at \( B \) so that it can turn freely in a vertical plane. The rod is balanced in the vertical position above the hinge and then slightly disturbed. Prove that the time taken to pass from the horizontal position to the lowest position is
\[
\sqrt{\frac{a}{g}} \log _{e}(1+\sqrt{2})
\]
solution:
First Solution. General existence theorems for linear differential equations assure us that there is a unique solution to the given system satisfying the initial conditions and that this solution is infinitely differentiable. Solve the first equation for \( y \) and then differentiate:
(1)
\[
y=\frac{d x}{d t}-x+3
\]
(2)
\[
\frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}-\frac{d x}{d t} .
\]

Now eliminate \( y \) from the second of the original equations:
(3)
\[
\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}-\frac{d x}{d t}=-2 x+3\left(\frac{d x}{d t}-x+3\right)+1
\]
whence
(4)
\[
\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}-4 \frac{d x}{d t}+5 x=10
\]

This equation has the obvious particular solution \( x=2 \) and the general solution
(5)
\[
x=e^{2 t}(A \cos t+B \sin t)+2 .
\]

The constants \( A \) and \( B \) can be evaluated from the initial conditions \( x=0 \) and \( d x / d t=-3 \) (derived from the given conditions and from the first of the original equations) when \( t=0 \). We find \( A=-2, B=+1 \), and therefore
(6)
\[
x=e^{2 t}(-2 \cos t+\sin t)+2 .
\]

From (1), we now obtain
\[
y=e^{2 t}(-\cos t+3 \sin t)+1 .
\]

It is easy to verify that equations (6) and (7) define solutions of the given system.

Second Solution. Treat the given system as a single differential equation in vectors
\[
\mathbf{x}^{\prime}=A \mathbf{x}+\mathbf{b}
\]
where
\[
x=\binom{x}{y}, \quad A=\left(\begin{array}{rr}
1 & 1 \\
-2 & 3
\end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad b=\binom{-3}{1} .
\]

Solving the equation \( A \mathbf{x}+\mathbf{b}=0 \), we find the constant particular solution \( \binom{2}{1} \). The general solution is therefore
\[
x=(\exp A t) c+\binom{2}{1}
\]
where \( \mathbf{c} \) is an arbitrary constant vector. Using the initial condition \( \boldsymbol{x}=0 \) when \( t=0 \), we find
\[
c=-\binom{2}{1}
\]

The characteristic polynomial of \( A \) is
\[
\operatorname{det}(A-u I)=\left|\begin{array}{cc}
1-u & 1 \\
-2 & 3-u
\end{array}\right|=(u-2)^{2}+1,
\]
where \( I \) is the identity matrix. Hence
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\qquad \begin{array}{l}
(A-2 I)^{2}=-I \\
\begin{aligned}
\exp (A-2 I) t= & 1+(A-2 I) t+\frac{(A-2 I)^{2}}{2!} t^{2}+\frac{(A-2 I)^{3}}{3!} t^{3}+\cdots \\
= & (\cos t) I+(\sin t)(A-2 I)
\end{aligned} \\
\text { and } \\
\quad \exp A t=e^{2 t}((\cos t) I+(\sin t)(A-2 I)) \\
\text { giving finally } \\
\qquad \mathbf{x}= e^{2 t}\left\{(\cos t)\binom{-2}{-1}+(\sin t)\binom{1}{3}\right\}+\binom{2}{1}
\end{array}
\end{array}
\]
which is equivalent to the two equations (6) and (7).
Solution. Let \( m \) be the mass of the particle, and let \( \theta \) be the angular position of the rod, measured from the vertical, at time \( t \). The force of gravity \( m g \) can be resolved into two components, \( m g \cos \theta \) acting along the rod, and \( m g \sin \theta \) acting perpendicular to the rod. The former is counterbalanced by the tension (or compression) in the rod and the latter accelerates the particle along the circle of radius \( a \). By Newton's third law we have
\[
m g \sin \theta=m a \frac{d^{2} \theta}{d t^{2}} .
\]

Multiply through by \( \frac{2}{m} \frac{d \theta}{d t} \) to get
\[
2 g \sin \theta \frac{d \theta}{d t}=2 a \frac{d \theta}{d t} \frac{d^{2} \theta}{d t^{2}} .
\]

This can be integrated to give
(1)
\[
-2 g \cos \theta+k=a\left(\frac{d \theta}{d t}\right)^{2} .
\]

From the initial conditions, \( \theta=d \theta / d t=0 \) when \( t=0 \), we find \( k=2 g \).
Thus we have
(2)
\[
a\left(\frac{d \theta}{d t}\right)^{2}=2 g(1-\cos \theta)=4 g \sin ^{2}(\theta / 2)
\]
whence
\[
\frac{d \theta}{d t}=2 \sqrt{(g / a)} \sin (\theta / 2) .
\]

We have chosen the positive square root because \( d \theta / d t \) is positive for \( 0<\theta \leq \pi \).
The time required for the passage from \( \theta=\pi / 2 \) to \( \theta=\pi \) is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \frac{d t}{d \theta} d \theta & =\int_{\pi^{\prime} 2}^{\pi} \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{a}{g}} \csc (\theta / 2) d \theta \\
& =\sqrt{\frac{a}{g}}[-\log (\csc (\theta / 2)+\cot (\theta / 2))]_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \\
& =\sqrt{\frac{a}{g}} \log (\sqrt{2}+1) .
\end{aligned}
\]

First Remark. By using the fact that the kinetic energy of the particle is equal to its loss of potential energy, we could start with the equation
\[
\frac{1}{2} m\left(a \frac{d \theta}{d t}\right)^{2}=m g a(1-\cos \theta)
\]
and obtain (2) directly.
Second Remark. We evaluated the constant of integration in (1) as if the particle fell from the very top of the circle, but actually no such motion is possible, as we can easily see by noting that the time required to fall from \( \theta=\epsilon \) to \( \theta=\pi \) approaches infinity as \( \epsilon \rightarrow 0 \). The precise result is that if \( T(\epsilon) \) is the time required to pass from the horizontal position to the lowest position when the particle starts at \( \theta=\epsilon \), then \( \lim _{\epsilon-0} T(\epsilon)=\sqrt{a / g} \) \( \log (\sqrt{2}+1) \). This follows because we can pass to the limit under the sign of integration in the formula
\[
T(\epsilon)=\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{a}{2 g}} \frac{d \theta}{\sqrt{\cos \epsilon-\cos \theta}} .
\]

descriptive_long.question:
Take either (i) or (ii).

(i) Solve the system of differential equations
\[
\frac{d coordx}{d timvar}=coordx+coordy-3,\quad
\frac{d coordy}{d timvar}=-2 coordx+3 coordy+1
\]
subject to the conditions \( coordx=coordy=0 \) for \( timvar=0 \).
(page 101)

(ii) A heavy particle is attached to the end \( matrixa \) of a light \( \operatorname{rod} matrixa matrixb \) of length \( rodleng \). The rod is hinged at \( matrixb \) so that it can turn freely in a vertical plane. The rod is balanced in the vertical position above the hinge and then slightly disturbed. Prove that the time taken to pass from the horizontal position to the lowest position is
\[
\sqrt{\frac{rodleng}{gravity}} \log _{e}(1+\sqrt{2})
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. General existence theorems for linear differential equations assure us that there is a unique solution to the given system satisfying the initial conditions and that this solution is infinitely differentiable. Solve the first equation for \( coordy \) and then differentiate:
(1)
\[
coordy=\frac{d coordx}{d timvar}-coordx+3
\]
(2)
\[
\frac{d coordy}{d timvar}=\frac{d^{2} coordx}{d timvar^{2}}-\frac{d coordx}{d timvar}
\]

Now eliminate \( coordy \) from the second of the original equations:
(3)
\[
\frac{d^{2} coordx}{d timvar^{2}}-\frac{d coordx}{d timvar}=-2 coordx+3\left(\frac{d coordx}{d timvar}-coordx+3\right)+1
\]
whence
(4)
\[
\frac{d^{2} coordx}{d timvar^{2}}-4 \frac{d coordx}{d timvar}+5 coordx=10
\]

This equation has the obvious particular solution \( coordx=2 \) and the general solution
(5)
\[
coordx=e^{2 timvar}(matrixa \cos timvar+matrixb \sin timvar)+2
\]

The constants \( matrixa \) and \( matrixb \) can be evaluated from the initial conditions \( coordx=0 \) and \( d coordx / d timvar=-3 \) (derived from the given conditions and from the first of the original equations) when \( timvar=0 \). We find \( matrixa=-2, matrixb=+1 \), and therefore
(6)
\[
coordx=e^{2 timvar}(-2 \cos timvar+\sin timvar)+2
\]

From (1), we now obtain
\[
coordy=e^{2 timvar}(-\cos timvar+3 \sin timvar)+1
\]

It is easy to verify that equations (6) and (7) define solutions of the given system.

Second Solution. Treat the given system as a single differential equation in vectors
\[
\mathbf{coordx}'=matrixa \mathbf{coordx}+vectorb
\]
where
\[
coordx=\binom{coordx}{coordy}, \quad matrixa=\begin{pmatrix}1 & 1\\-2 & 3\end{pmatrix} \quad \text{and} \quad vectorb=\binom{-3}{1}
\]

Solving the equation \( matrixa \mathbf{coordx}+vectorb=0 \), we find the constant particular solution \( \binom{2}{1} \). The general solution is therefore
\[
coordx=(\exp matrixa timvar) constc+\binom{2}{1}
\]
where \( \mathbf{constc} \) is an arbitrary constant vector. Using the initial condition \( \boldsymbol{coordx}=0 \) when \( timvar=0 \), we find
\[
constc=-\binom{2}{1}
\]

The characteristic polynomial of \( matrixa \) is
\[
\det(matrixa-eigenu identity)=\left|\begin{array}{cc}1-eigenu & 1\\-2 & 3-eigenu\end{array}\right|=(eigenu-2)^{2}+1,
\]
where \( identity \) is the identity matrix. Hence
\[
(matrixa-2 identity)^{2}=-identity,\quad \exp((matrixa-2 identity) timvar)=1+(matrixa-2 identity) timvar+\frac{(matrixa-2 identity)^{2}}{2!} timvar^{2}+\frac{(matrixa-2 identity)^{3}}{3!} timvar^{3}+\cdots=(\cos timvar) identity+(\sin timvar)(matrixa-2 identity),
\]
so
\[
\exp(matrixa timvar)=e^{2 timvar}\big((\cos timvar) identity+(\sin timvar)(matrixa-2 identity)\big),
\]
which finally gives
\[
\mathbf{coordx}=e^{2 timvar}\big\{(\cos timvar)\binom{-2}{-1}+(\sin timvar)\binom{1}{3}\big\}+\binom{2}{1}
\]
which is equivalent to the two equations (6) and (7).

Solution. Let \( masspar \) be the mass of the particle, and let \( angleth \) be the angular position of the rod, measured from the vertical, at time \( timvar \). The force of gravity \( masspar gravity \) can be resolved into two components, \( masspar gravity \cos angleth \) acting along the rod, and \( masspar gravity \sin angleth \) acting perpendicular to the rod. The former is counterbalanced by the tension (or compression) in the rod and the latter accelerates the particle along the circle of radius \( rodleng \). By Newton's third law we have
\[
masspar gravity \sin angleth = masspar rodleng \frac{d^{2} angleth}{d timvar^{2}}
\]

Multiply through by \( \frac{2}{masspar} \frac{d angleth}{d timvar} \) to get
\[
2 gravity \sin angleth \frac{d angleth}{d timvar}=2 rodleng \frac{d angleth}{d timvar} \frac{d^{2} angleth}{d timvar^{2}}
\]

This can be integrated to give
(1)
\[
-2 gravity \cos angleth+constk=rodleng\left(\frac{d angleth}{d timvar}\right)^{2}
\]

From the initial conditions, \( angleth=d angleth / d timvar=0 \) when \( timvar=0 \), we find \( constk=2 gravity \).
Thus we have
(2)
\[
rodleng\left(\frac{d angleth}{d timvar}\right)^{2}=2 gravity(1-\cos angleth)=4 gravity \sin^{2}(angleth/2)
\]
whence
\[
\frac{d angleth}{d timvar}=2 \sqrt{gravity/rodleng} \sin(angleth/2)
\]

We choose the positive square root because \( \frac{d angleth}{d timvar} > 0 \) for \( 0<angleth \le \pi \).
The time required for the passage from \( angleth=\pi/2 \) to \( angleth=\pi \) is
\[
\int_{\pi/2}^{\pi} \frac{d timvar}{d angleth} d angleth=\int_{\pi/2}^{\pi} \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{rodleng}{gravity}} \csc(angleth/2) d angleth=\sqrt{\frac{rodleng}{gravity}}\,[-\log(\csc(angleth/2)+\cot(angleth/2))]_{\pi/2}^{\pi}=\sqrt{\frac{rodleng}{gravity}} \log(\sqrt{2}+1)
\]

First Remark. Using energy conservation, we could start with
\[
\frac{1}{2} masspar\left(rodleng \frac{d angleth}{d timvar}\right)^{2}=masspar gravity rodleng(1-\cos angleth)
\]
and obtain (2) directly.

Second Remark. We evaluated the constant of integration in (1) as if the particle fell from the very top of the circle, but actually no such motion is possible, as we can see by noting that the time required to fall from \( angleth=epsivar \) to \( angleth=\pi \) approaches infinity as \( epsivar \to 0 \). The precise result is that if \( timefun(epsivar) \) is the time required to pass from the horizontal position to the lowest position when the particle starts at \( angleth=epsivar \), then
\[
\lim_{epsivar \to 0} timefun(epsivar)=\sqrt{rodleng/gravity}\,\log(\sqrt{2}+1).
\]
This follows because we can pass to the limit under the sign of integration in
\[
timefun(epsivar)=\int_{\pi/2}^{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{rodleng}{2 gravity}} \frac{d angleth}{\sqrt{\cos epsivar-\cos angleth}}.
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
5. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) Solve the system of differential equations
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{d aftershock}{d hummingbird}=aftershock+snowflake-3 \\
\frac{d snowflake}{d hummingbird}=-2 aftershock+3 snowflake+1
\end{array}
\]
subject to the conditions \( aftershock=snowflake=0 \) for \( hummingbird=0 \).
(page 101)
(ii) A heavy particle is attached to the end \( playhouse \) of a light \( \operatorname{rod} playhouse moonstone \) of length \( waterfall \). The rod is hinged at \( moonstone \) so that it can turn freely in a vertical plane. The rod is balanced in the vertical position above the hinge and then slightly disturbed. Prove that the time taken to pass from the horizontal position to the lowest position is
\[
\sqrt{\frac{waterfall}{starlight}} \log _{e}(1+\sqrt{2})
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. General existence theorems for linear differential equations assure us that there is a unique solution to the given system satisfying the initial conditions and that this solution is infinitely differentiable. Solve the first equation for \( snowflake \) and then differentiate:
(1)
\[
 snowflake=\frac{d aftershock}{d hummingbird}-aftershock+3
\]
(2)
\[
\frac{d snowflake}{d hummingbird}=\frac{d^{2} aftershock}{d hummingbird^{2}}-\frac{d aftershock}{d hummingbird} .
\]

Now eliminate \( snowflake \) from the second of the original equations:
(3)
\[
\frac{d^{2} aftershock}{d hummingbird^{2}}-\frac{d aftershock}{d hummingbird}=-2 aftershock+3\left(\frac{d aftershock}{d hummingbird}-aftershock+3\right)+1
\]
whence
(4)
\[
\frac{d^{2} aftershock}{d hummingbird^{2}}-4 \frac{d aftershock}{d hummingbird}+5 aftershock=10
\]

This equation has the obvious particular solution \( aftershock=2 \) and the general solution
(5)
\[
aftershock=e^{2 hummingbird}(playhouse \cos hummingbird+moonstone \sin hummingbird)+2 .
\]

The constants \( playhouse \) and \( moonstone \) can be evaluated from the initial conditions \( aftershock=0 \) and \( d aftershock / d hummingbird=-3 \) (derived from the given conditions and from the first of the original equations) when \( hummingbird=0 \). We find \( playhouse=-2, moonstone=+1 \), and therefore
(6)
\[
aftershock=e^{2 hummingbird}(-2 \cos hummingbird+\sin hummingbird)+2 .
\]

From (1), we now obtain
\[
snowflake=e^{2 hummingbird}(-\cos hummingbird+3 \sin hummingbird)+1 .
\]

It is easy to verify that equations (6) and (7) define solutions of the given system.

Second Solution. Treat the given system as a single differential equation in vectors
\[
\mathbf{aftershock}^{\prime}=playhouse \mathbf{aftershock}+\mathbf{sandstone}
\]
where
\[
aftershock=\binom{aftershock}{snowflake}, \quad playhouse=\left(\begin{array}{rr}
1 & 1 \\
-2 & 3
\end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad sandstone=\binom{-3}{1} .
\]

Solving the equation \( playhouse \mathbf{aftershock}+\mathbf{sandstone}=0 \), we find the constant particular solution \( \binom{2}{1} \). The general solution is therefore
\[
aftershock=(\exp playhouse\,hummingbird) blueberry+\binom{2}{1}
\]
where \( \mathbf{blueberry} \) is an arbitrary constant vector. Using the initial condition \( \boldsymbol{aftershock}=0 \) when \( hummingbird=0 \), we find
\[
blueberry=-\binom{2}{1}
\]

The characteristic polynomial of playhouse is
\[
\operatorname{det}(playhouse-labyrinth\ lighthouse)=\left|\begin{array}{cc}
1-labyrinth & 1 \\
-2 & 3-labyrinth
\end{array}\right|=(labyrinth-2)^{2}+1,
\]
where \( lighthouse \) is the identity matrix. Hence
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\qquad \begin{array}{l}
(playhouse-2 lighthouse)^{2}=-lighthouse \\
\begin{aligned}
\exp (playhouse-2 lighthouse)\,hummingbird= & 1+(playhouse-2 lighthouse)\,hummingbird+\frac{(playhouse-2 lighthouse)^{2}}{2!} hummingbird^{2}+\frac{(playhouse-2 lighthouse)^{3}}{3!} hummingbird^{3}+\cdots \\
= & (\cos hummingbird) lighthouse+(\sin hummingbird)(playhouse-2 lighthouse)
\end{aligned} \\
\text { and } \\
\quad \exp playhouse\,hummingbird=e^{2 hummingbird}((\cos hummingbird) lighthouse+(\sin hummingbird)(playhouse-2 lighthouse)) \\
\text { giving finally } \\
\qquad \mathbf{aftershock}= e^{2 hummingbird}\left\{(\cos hummingbird)\binom{-2}{-1}+(\sin hummingbird)\binom{1}{3}\right\}+\binom{2}{1}
\end{array}
\end{array}
\]
which is equivalent to the two equations (6) and (7).

Solution. Let \( castleton \) be the mass of the particle, and let \( marigold \) be the angular position of the rod, measured from the vertical, at time \( hummingbird \). The force of gravity \( castleton starlight \) can be resolved into two components, \( castleton starlight \cos marigold \) acting along the rod, and \( castleton starlight \sin marigold \) acting perpendicular to the rod. The former is counterbalanced by the tension (or compression) in the rod and the latter accelerates the particle along the circle of radius \( waterfall \). By Newton's third law we have
\[
castleton starlight \sin marigold=castleton waterfall \frac{d^{2} marigold}{d hummingbird^{2}} .
\]

Multiply through by \( \frac{2}{castleton} \frac{d marigold}{d hummingbird} \) to get
\[
2 starlight \sin marigold \frac{d marigold}{d hummingbird}=2 waterfall \frac{d marigold}{d hummingbird} \frac{d^{2} marigold}{d hummingbird^{2}} .
\]

This can be integrated to give
(1)
\[
-2 starlight \cos marigold+rainstorm=waterfall\left(\frac{d marigold}{d hummingbird}\right)^{2} .
\]

From the initial conditions, \( marigold=d marigold / d hummingbird=0 \) when \( hummingbird=0 \), we find \( rainstorm=2 starlight \).
Thus we have
(2)
\[
waterfall\left(\frac{d marigold}{d hummingbird}\right)^{2}=2 starlight(1-\cos marigold)=4 starlight \sin ^{2}(marigold / 2)
\]
whence
\[
\frac{d marigold}{d hummingbird}=2 \sqrt{(starlight / waterfall)} \sin (marigold / 2) .
\]

We have chosen the positive square root because \( d marigold / d hummingbird \) is positive for \( 0<marigold \leq \pi \).
The time required for the passage from \( marigold=\pi / 2 \) to \( marigold=\pi \) is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \frac{d hummingbird}{d marigold} d marigold & =\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{waterfall}{starlight}} \csc (marigold / 2) d marigold \\
& =\sqrt{\frac{waterfall}{starlight}}[-\log (\csc (marigold / 2)+\cot (marigold / 2))]_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \\
& =\sqrt{\frac{waterfall}{starlight}} \log (\sqrt{2}+1) .
\end{aligned}
\]

First Remark. By using the fact that the kinetic energy of the particle is equal to its loss of potential energy, we could start with the equation
\[
\frac{1}{2} castleton\left(waterfall \frac{d marigold}{d hummingbird}\right)^{2}=castleton starlight waterfall(1-\cos marigold)
\]
and obtain (2) directly.
Second Remark. We evaluated the constant of integration in (1) as if the particle fell from the very top of the circle, but actually no such motion is possible, as we can easily see by noting that the time required to fall from \( marigold=wanderlust \) to \( marigold=\pi \) approaches infinity as \( wanderlust \rightarrow 0 \). The precise result is that if \( buttercup(wanderlust) \) is the time required to pass from the horizontal position to the lowest position when the particle starts at \( marigold=wanderlust \), then \( \lim _{wanderlust-0} buttercup(wanderlust)=\sqrt{waterfall / starlight} \log (\sqrt{2}+1) \). This follows because we can pass to the limit under the sign of integration in the formula
\[
buttercup(wanderlust)=\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{waterfall}{2 starlight}} \frac{d marigold}{\sqrt{\cos wanderlust-\cos marigold}} .
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem:
<<<
5. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) Solve the system of differential equations
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{d fixedcoordinate}{d spacedimension}=fixedcoordinate+staticordinate-3 \\
\frac{d staticordinate}{d spacedimension}=-2 fixedcoordinate+3 staticordinate+1
\end{array}
\]
subject to the conditions \( fixedcoordinate=staticordinate=0 \) for \( spacedimension=0 \).
(page 101)
(ii) A heavy particle is attached to the end \( wanderarray \) of a light \( \operatorname{rod} wanderarray mutablefactor \) of length \( shortness \). The rod is hinged at \( mutablefactor \) so that it can turn freely in a vertical plane. The rod is balanced in the vertical position above the hinge and then slightly disturbed. Prove that the time taken to pass from the horizontal position to the lowest position is
\[
\sqrt{\frac{shortness}{levitationrate}} \log _{e}(1+\sqrt{2})
\]
>>>
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. General existence theorems for linear differential equations assure us that there is a unique solution to the given system satisfying the initial conditions and that this solution is infinitely differentiable. Solve the first equation for \( staticordinate \) and then differentiate:
(1)
\[
staticordinate=\frac{d fixedcoordinate}{d spacedimension}-fixedcoordinate+3
\]
(2)
\[
\frac{d staticordinate}{d spacedimension}=\frac{d^{2} fixedcoordinate}{d spacedimension^{2}}-\frac{d fixedcoordinate}{d spacedimension} .
\]

Now eliminate \( staticordinate \) from the second of the original equations:
(3)
\[
\frac{d^{2} fixedcoordinate}{d spacedimension^{2}}-\frac{d fixedcoordinate}{d spacedimension}=-2 fixedcoordinate+3\left(\frac{d fixedcoordinate}{d spacedimension}-fixedcoordinate+3\right)+1
\]
whence
(4)
\[
\frac{d^{2} fixedcoordinate}{d spacedimension^{2}}-4 \frac{d fixedcoordinate}{d spacedimension}+5 fixedcoordinate=10
\]

This equation has the obvious particular solution \( fixedcoordinate=2 \) and the general solution
(5)
\[
fixedcoordinate=e^{2 spacedimension}(wanderarray \cos spacedimension+mutablefactor \sin spacedimension)+2 .
\]

The constants \( wanderarray \) and \( mutablefactor \) can be evaluated from the initial conditions \( fixedcoordinate=0 \) and \( d fixedcoordinate / d spacedimension=-3 \) (derived from the given conditions and from the first of the original equations) when \( spacedimension=0 \). We find \( wanderarray=-2, mutablefactor=+1 \), and therefore
(6)
\[
fixedcoordinate=e^{2 spacedimension}(-2 \cos spacedimension+\sin spacedimension)+2 .
\]

From (1), we now obtain
\[
staticordinate=e^{2 spacedimension}(-\cos spacedimension+3 \sin spacedimension)+1 .
\]

It is easy to verify that equations (6) and (7) define solutions of the given system.

Second Solution. Treat the given system as a single differential equation in vectors
\[
\mathbf{fixedcoordinate}^{\prime}=wanderarray \mathbf{fixedcoordinate}+\mathbf{shiftingvector}
\]
where
\[
fixedcoordinate=\binom{fixedcoordinate}{staticordinate}, \quad wanderarray=\left(\begin{array}{rr}
1 & 1 \\
-2 & 3
\end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad shiftingvector=\binom{-3}{1} .
\]

Solving the equation \( wanderarray \mathbf{fixedcoordinate}+\mathbf{shiftingvector}=0 \), we find the constant particular solution \( \binom{2}{1} \). The general solution is therefore
\[
fixedcoordinate=(\exp wanderarray spacedimension) changingvector+\binom{2}{1}
\]
where \( \mathbf{changingvector} \) is an arbitrary constant vector. Using the initial condition \( \boldsymbol{fixedcoordinate}=0 \) when \( spacedimension=0 \), we find
\[
changingvector=-\binom{2}{1}
\]

The characteristic polynomial of \( wanderarray \) is
\[
\operatorname{det}(wanderarray-constantvalue zeromatrix)=\left|\begin{array}{cc}
1-constantvalue & 1 \\
-2 & 3-constantvalue
\end{array}\right|=(constantvalue-2)^{2}+1,
\]
where \( zeromatrix \) is the identity matrix. Hence
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\qquad \begin{array}{l}
(wanderarray-2 zeromatrix)^{2}=-zeromatrix \\
\begin{aligned}
\exp (wanderarray-2 zeromatrix) spacedimension= & 1+(wanderarray-2 zeromatrix) spacedimension+\frac{(wanderarray-2 zeromatrix)^{2}}{2!} spacedimension^{2}+\frac{(wanderarray-2 zeromatrix)^{3}}{3!} spacedimension^{3}+\cdots \\
= & (\cos spacedimension) zeromatrix+(\sin spacedimension)(wanderarray-2 zeromatrix)
\end{aligned} \\
\text { and } \\
\quad \exp wanderarray spacedimension=e^{2 spacedimension}((\cos spacedimension) zeromatrix+(\sin spacedimension)(wanderarray-2 zeromatrix)) \\
\text { giving finally } \\
\qquad \mathbf{fixedcoordinate}= e^{2 spacedimension}\left\{(\cos spacedimension)\binom{-2}{-1}+(\sin spacedimension)\binom{1}{3}\right\}+\binom{2}{1}
\end{array}
\end{array}
\]
which is equivalent to the two equations (6) and (7).
Solution. Let \( masslessness \) be the mass of the particle, and let \( straightmeasure \) be the angular position of the rod, measured from the vertical, at time \( spacedimension \). The force of gravity \( masslessness levitationrate \) can be resolved into two components, \( masslessness levitationrate \cos straightmeasure \) acting along the rod, and \( masslessness levitationrate \sin straightmeasure \) acting perpendicular to the rod. The former is counterbalanced by the tension (or compression) in the rod and the latter accelerates the particle along the circle of radius \( shortness \). By Newton's third law we have
\[
masslessness levitationrate \sin straightmeasure=masslessness shortness \frac{d^{2} straightmeasure}{d spacedimension^{2}} .
\]

Multiply through by \( \frac{2}{masslessness} \frac{d straightmeasure}{d spacedimension} \) to get
\[
2 levitationrate \sin straightmeasure \frac{d straightmeasure}{d spacedimension}=2 shortness \frac{d straightmeasure}{d spacedimension} \frac{d^{2} straightmeasure}{d spacedimension^{2}} .
\]

This can be integrated to give
(1)
\[
-2 levitationrate \cos straightmeasure+variableness=shortness\left(\frac{d straightmeasure}{d spacedimension}\right)^{2} .
\]

From the initial conditions, \( straightmeasure=d straightmeasure / d spacedimension=0 \) when \( spacedimension=0 \), we find \( variableness=2 levitationrate \).
Thus we have
(2)
\[
shortness\left(\frac{d straightmeasure}{d spacedimension}\right)^{2}=2 levitationrate(1-\cos straightmeasure)=4 levitationrate \sin ^{2}(straightmeasure / 2)
\]
whence
\[
\frac{d straightmeasure}{d spacedimension}=2 \sqrt{(levitationrate / shortness)} \sin (straightmeasure / 2) .
\]

We have chosen the positive square root because \( d straightmeasure / d spacedimension \) is positive for \( 0<straightmeasure \leq \pi \).
The time required for the passage from \( straightmeasure=\pi / 2 \) to \( straightmeasure=\pi \) is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \frac{d spacedimension}{d straightmeasure} d straightmeasure & =\int_{\pi^{\prime} 2}^{\pi} \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{shortness}{levitationrate}} \csc (straightmeasure / 2) d straightmeasure \\
& =\sqrt{\frac{shortness}{levitationrate}}[-\log (\csc (straightmeasure / 2)+\cot (straightmeasure / 2))]_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \\
& =\sqrt{\frac{shortness}{levitationrate}} \log (\sqrt{2}+1) .
\end{aligned}
\]

First Remark. By using the fact that the kinetic energy of the particle is equal to its loss of potential energy, we could start with the equation
\[
\frac{1}{2} masslessness\left(shortness \frac{d straightmeasure}{d spacedimension}\right)^{2}=masslessness levitationrate shortness(1-\cos straightmeasure)
\]
and obtain (2) directly.
Second Remark. We evaluated the constant of integration in (1) as if the particle fell from the very top of the circle, but actually no such motion is possible, as we can easily see by noting that the time required to fall from \( straightmeasure=largedeviation \) to \( straightmeasure=\pi \) approaches infinity as \( largedeviation \rightarrow 0 \). The precise result is that if \( spaceinterval(largedeviation) \) is the time required to pass from the horizontal position to the lowest position when the particle starts at \( straightmeasure=largedeviation \), then \( \lim _{largedeviation-0} spaceinterval(largedeviation)=\sqrt{shortness / levitationrate} \) \( \log (\sqrt{2}+1) \). This follows because we can pass to the limit under the sign of integration in the formula
\[
spaceinterval(largedeviation)=\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{shortness}{2 levitationrate}} \frac{d straightmeasure}{\sqrt{\cos largedeviation-\cos straightmeasure}} .
\]

garbled_string.question:
5. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) Solve the system of differential equations
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{d qzxwvtnp}{d mbvdyncq}=qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla-3 \\
\frac{d hjgrksla}{d mbvdyncq}=-2 qzxwvtnp+3 hjgrksla+1
\end{array}
\]
subject to the conditions \( qzxwvtnp=hjgrksla=0 \) for \( mbvdyncq=0 \).
(page 101)
(ii) A heavy particle is attached to the end \( fcovrehn \) of a light \( \operatorname{rod} fcovrehn jkslqpze \) of length \( srujdvke \). The rod is hinged at \( jkslqpze \) so that it can turn freely in a vertical plane. The rod is balanced in the vertical position above the hinge and then slightly disturbed. Prove that the time taken to pass from the horizontal position to the lowest position is
\[
\sqrt{\frac{srujdvke}{ylmiztch}} \log _{e}(1+\sqrt{2})
\]
garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. General existence theorems for linear differential equations assure us that there is a unique solution to the given system satisfying the initial conditions and that this solution is infinitely differentiable. Solve the first equation for \( hjgrksla \) and then differentiate:
(1)
\[
hjgrksla=\frac{d qzxwvtnp}{d mbvdyncq}-qzxwvtnp+3
\]
(2)
\[
\frac{d hjgrksla}{d mbvdyncq}=\frac{d^{2} qzxwvtnp}{d mbvdyncq^{2}}-\frac{d qzxwvtnp}{d mbvdyncq} .
\]

Now eliminate \( hjgrksla \) from the second of the original equations:
(3)
\[
\frac{d^{2} qzxwvtnp}{d mbvdyncq^{2}}-\frac{d qzxwvtnp}{d mbvdyncq}=-2 qzxwvtnp+3\left(\frac{d qzxwvtnp}{d mbvdyncq}-qzxwvtnp+3\right)+1
\]
whence
(4)
\[
\frac{d^{2} qzxwvtnp}{d mbvdyncq^{2}}-4 \frac{d qzxwvtnp}{d mbvdyncq}+5 qzxwvtnp=10
\]

This equation has the obvious particular solution \( qzxwvtnp=2 \) and the general solution
(5)
\[
qzxwvtnp=e^{2 mbvdyncq}(fcovrehn \cos mbvdyncq+jkslqpze \sin mbvdyncq)+2 .
\]

The constants \( fcovrehn \) and \( jkslqpze \) can be evaluated from the initial conditions \( qzxwvtnp=0 \) and \( d qzxwvtnp / d mbvdyncq=-3 \) (derived from the given conditions and from the first of the original equations) when \( mbvdyncq=0 \). We find \( fcovrehn=-2, jkslqpze=+1 \), and therefore
(6)
\[
qzxwvtnp=e^{2 mbvdyncq}(-2 \cos mbvdyncq+\sin mbvdyncq)+2 .
\]

From (1), we now obtain
\[
hjgrksla=e^{2 mbvdyncq}(-\cos mbvdyncq+3 \sin mbvdyncq)+1 .
\]

It is easy to verify that equations (6) and (7) define solutions of the given system.

Second Solution. Treat the given system as a single differential equation in vectors
\[
\mathbf{qzxwvtnp}^{\prime}=fcovrehn \mathbf{qzxwvtnp}+afnought
\]
where
\[
\mathbf{qzxwvtnp}=\binom{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla}, \quad fcovrehn=\left(\begin{array}{rr}1 & 1 \\ -2 & 3\end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad afnought=\binom{-3}{1} .
\]

Solving the equation \( fcovrehn \mathbf{qzxwvtnp}+afnought=0 \), we find the constant particular solution \( \binom{2}{1} \). The general solution is therefore
\[
\mathbf{qzxwvtnp}=(\exp fcovrehn mbvdyncq) xesruioq+\binom{2}{1}
\]
where \( \mathbf{xesruioq} \) is an arbitrary constant vector. Using the initial condition \( \boldsymbol{qzxwvtnp}=0 \) when \( mbvdyncq=0 \), we find
\[
xesruioq=-\binom{2}{1}
\]

The characteristic polynomial of \( fcovrehn \) is
\[
\operatorname{det}(fcovrehn-vibsrnje qrtmxvia)=\left|\begin{array}{cc}1-vibsrnje & 1 \\ -2 & 3-vibsrnje\end{array}\right|=(vibsrnje-2)^{2}+1,
\]
where \( qrtmxvia \) is the identity matrix. Hence
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\qquad \begin{array}{l}
(fcovrehn-2 qrtmxvia)^{2}=-qrtmxvia \\
\begin{aligned}
\exp (fcovrehn-2 qrtmxvia) mbvdyncq= & 1+(fcovrehn-2 qrtmxvia) mbvdyncq+\frac{(fcovrehn-2 qrtmxvia)^{2}}{2!} mbvdyncq^{2}+\frac{(fcovrehn-2 qrtmxvia)^{3}}{3!} mbvdyncq^{3}+\cdots \\
= & (\cos mbvdyncq) qrtmxvia+(\sin mbvdyncq)(fcovrehn-2 qrtmxvia)
\end{aligned} \\
\text { and } \\
\quad \exp fcovrehn mbvdyncq=e^{2 mbvdyncq}((\cos mbvdyncq) qrtmxvia+(\sin mbvdyncq)(fcovrehn-2 qrtmxvia)) \\
\text { giving finally } \\
\qquad \mathbf{qzxwvtnp}= e^{2 mbvdyncq}\left\{(\cos mbvdyncq)\binom{-2}{-1}+(\sin mbvdyncq)\binom{1}{3}\right\}+\binom{2}{1}
\end{array}
\end{array}
\]
which is equivalent to the two equations (6) and (7).
Solution. Let \( zqrtbwol \) be the mass of the particle, and let \( ploqjems \) be the angular position of the rod, measured from the vertical, at time \( mbvdyncq \). The force of gravity \( zqrtbwol ylmiztch \) can be resolved into two components, \( zqrtbwol ylmiztch \cos ploqjems \) acting along the rod, and \( zqrtbwol ylmiztch \sin ploqjems \) acting perpendicular to the rod. The former is counterbalanced by the tension (or compression) in the rod and the latter accelerates the particle along the circle of radius \( srujdvke \). By Newton's third law we have
\[
zqrtbwol ylmiztch \sin ploqjems=zqrtbwol srujdvke \frac{d^{2} ploqjems}{d mbvdyncq^{2}} .
\]

Multiply through by \( \frac{2}{zqrtbwol} \frac{d ploqjems}{d mbvdyncq} \) to get
\[
2 ylmiztch \sin ploqjems \frac{d ploqjems}{d mbvdyncq}=2 srujdvke \frac{d ploqjems}{d mbvdyncq} \frac{d^{2} ploqjems}{d mbvdyncq^{2}} .
\]

This can be integrated to give
(1)
\[
-2 ylmiztch \cos ploqjems+udmgpxye=srujdvke\left(\frac{d ploqjems}{d mbvdyncq}\right)^{2} .
\]

From the initial conditions, \( ploqjems=d ploqjems / d mbvdyncq=0 \) when \( mbvdyncq=0 \), we find \( udmgpxye=2 ylmiztch \).
Thus we have
(2)
\[
srujdvke\left(\frac{d ploqjems}{d mbvdyncq}\right)^{2}=2 ylmiztch(1-\cos ploqjems)=4 ylmiztch \sin ^{2}(ploqjems / 2)
\]
whence
\[
\frac{d ploqjems}{d mbvdyncq}=2 \sqrt{(ylmiztch / srujdvke)} \sin (ploqjems / 2) .
\]

We have chosen the positive square root because \( d ploqjems / d mbvdyncq \) is positive for \( 0<ploqjems \leq \pi \).
The time required for the passage from \( ploqjems=\pi / 2 \) to \( ploqjems=\pi \) is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \frac{d mbvdyncq}{d ploqjems} d ploqjems & =\int_{\pi^{\prime} 2}^{\pi} \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{srujdvke}{ylmiztch}} \csc (ploqjems / 2) d ploqjems \\
& =\sqrt{\frac{srujdvke}{ylmiztch}}[-\log (\csc (ploqjems / 2)+\cot (ploqjems / 2))]_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \\
& =\sqrt{\frac{srujdvke}{ylmiztch}} \log (\sqrt{2}+1) .
\end{aligned}
\]

First Remark. By using the fact that the kinetic energy of the particle is equal to its loss of potential energy, we could start with the equation
\[
\frac{1}{2} zqrtbwol\left(srujdvke \frac{d ploqjems}{d mbvdyncq}\right)^{2}=zqrtbwol ylmiztch srujdvke(1-\cos ploqjems)
\]
and obtain (2) directly.
Second Remark. We evaluated the constant of integration in (1) as if the particle fell from the very top of the circle, but actually no such motion is possible, as we can easily see by noting that the time required to fall from \( ploqjems=rfaxnque \) to \( ploqjems=\pi \) approaches infinity as \( rfaxnque \rightarrow 0 \). The precise result is that if \( wdumcoya(rfaxnque) \) is the time required to pass from the horizontal position to the lowest position when the particle starts at \( ploqjems=rfaxnque \), then \( \lim _{rfaxnque-0} wdumcoya(rfaxnque)=\sqrt{srujdvke / ylmiztch} \) \( \log (\sqrt{2}+1) \). This follows because we can pass to the limit under the sign of integration in the formula
\[
wdumcoya(rfaxnque)=\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{srujdvke}{2 ylmiztch}} \frac{d ploqjems}{\sqrt{\cos rfaxnque-\cos ploqjems}} .
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Take either (i) or (ii).

(i)  A resonant 3-dimensional, non-homogeneous Jordan-type system  
Solve the initial-value problem  
  dx/dt = 2x + y + z + (t+1)e^{2t} + sin t,  
  dy/dt = 2y + z + (t^{2}+t)e^{2t} + 2 cos t,  
  dz/dt = 2z        +               e^{2t} + 3 sin t,  
subject to the data x(0)=1, y(0)=0, z(0)=-1.  
(Observe that the linear part has matrix   
A = 2 1 1; 0 2 1; 0 0 2, i.e. 2I plus a nilpotent matrix with two super-diagonals.)

(ii)  A compound pendulum with distributed mass  
A uniform thin rod AB of length \ell  and mass m is hinged at B.  A point mass \mu  is bolted to the lower end A.  After being perfectly balanced in the upward vertical position the system receives an infinitesimal disturbance.  Prove that the time taken for the rod to pass from the configuration in which it makes an angle of 60^\circ with the upward vertical to the lowest position is  

  T = \sqrt{ \ell ( \mu  + m/3 ) / [ g( \mu  + m/2 ) ] } \cdot  log(2+\sqrt{3}).
kernel_variant.solution:
We give complete solutions of both parts; the reader may stop after either.

  
Solution to (i)

1.  Matrix form  
Write X(t)=x,y,z^{T}.  The system is  

 X' = AX + F(t),  A = 2I + N,    
 N = 0 1 1; 0 0 1; 0 0 0,  N^{3}=0,  

 F(t)=f_1,f_2,f_3^{T},    
 f_1(t)=(t+1)e^{2t}+sin t, f_2(t)=(t^{2}+t)e^{2t}+2 cos t, f_3(t)=e^{2t}+3 sin t.

Although N is not the standard single Jordan block (because of the (1,3)-entry 1), it is still strictly upper-triangular and nilpotent of order 3.

2.  Factor out the resonance exponential  
Put X(t)=e^{2t}U(t).  Then

 X' = 2e^{2t}U + e^{2t}U' = AX + F  
   = (2I+N)e^{2t}U + F.  

Cancelling 2e^{2t}U gives the triangular inhomogeneous system  

 U' = NU + G(t),  G(t)=e^{-2t}F(t),

where  

 g_1(t)=t+1+e^{-2t}sin t,  
 g_2(t)=t^{2}+t+2e^{-2t}cos t,  
 g_3(t)=1+3e^{-2t}sin t.

Write U=(u_1,u_2,u_3)^{T}; then  

 u_3' = g_3,  
 u_2' = u_3 + g_2,  
 u_1' = u_2 + u_3 + g_1                  (the extra ``+u_3'' comes from the (1,3)-entry of N).

Initial data: u(0)=X(0)=(1,0,-1).

3.  Integrate from the bottom upward  

(a)  u_3(t)  
 u_3(t)=-1 + \int _{0}^{t}[1+3e^{-2s}sin s]ds  
    = t - 2/5 - (3/5)e^{-2t}(2 sin t + cos t).

(b)  u_2(t)  
 u_2(t)= \int _{0}^{t}[u_3(s)+g_2(s)]ds  
    = t^{3}/3 + t^{2} - (2/5)t + 8/25  
     + (1/25)e^{-2t}(19 sin t - 8 cos t).

(c)  u_1(t)  
 u_1(t)=1 + \int _{0}^{t}[u_2(s)+u_3(s)+g_1(s)]ds  
    = t^{4}/12 + t^{3}/3 + (4/5)t^{2} + (23/25)t + 93/125  
     + e^{-2t}( -51/125 sin t + 32/125 cos t).

4.  Return to x,y,z  
Finally X(t)=e^{2t}U(t):

 z(t)=e^{2t}\Bigl[\,t - 2/5 - (3/5)e^{-2t}(2 sin t + cos t)\Bigr],  

 y(t)=e^{2t}\Bigl[\,t^{3}/3 + t^{2} - (2/5)t + 8/25  
        + (1/25)e^{-2t}(19 sin t - 8 cos t)\Bigr],  

 x(t)=e^{2t}\Bigl[\,t^{4}/12 + t^{3}/3 + (4/5)t^{2} + (23/25)t + 93/125  
        + e^{-2t}( -51/125 sin t + 32/125 cos t)\Bigr].  

Direct substitution shows that these expressions satisfy both the differential equations and the initial conditions.

  
Solution to (ii)

1.  Inertia and effective mass  
Let \theta  be the angle with the upward vertical.  
Moment of inertia about the hinge:

 I = I_rod + \mu \ell ^2 = (1/3)m\ell ^2 + \mu \ell ^2 = \ell ^2( \mu  + m/3 ).

The gravitational torque about B is -M g \ell  sin \theta , where  

 M = \mu  + m/2

combines the point mass \mu  at A and the centre of mass of the rod at \ell /2.

2.  Energy integral  
Release is from rest, so

 \frac{1}{2}I \theta ^{2} = M g \ell  (1-cos \theta ).     (*)

3.  First-order equation for \theta   
Using 1-cos \theta  = 2 sin^2(\theta /2) we obtain  

 \theta  = 2\sqrt{g \ell  M/I} \cdot  sin(\theta /2).     (\dagger )

4.  Time from 60^\circ to the lowest point  
Let \theta _0 = 60^\circ = \pi /3.  Then

 T = \int _{\pi /3}^{\pi } d\theta  / \theta   
   = (1/2)\sqrt{I/(g \ell  M)} \int _{\pi /3}^{\pi } \! csc(\theta /2)\,d\theta .

Put u = \theta /2 (du = d\theta /2); limits u: \pi /6 \to  \pi /2.  Hence

 T = \sqrt{I/(g \ell  M)} \int _{\pi /6}^{\pi /2} csc u \,du  
   = \sqrt{I/(g \ell  M)} [ -ln(csc u + cot u) ]_{\pi /6}^{\pi /2}  
   = \sqrt{I/(g \ell  M)} \cdot  ln(2 + \sqrt{3}).

Finally substitute I and M:

 T = \sqrt{ \ell ( \mu  + m/3 ) / [ g( \mu  + m/2 ) ] } \cdot  ln(2 + \sqrt{3}),

exactly as claimed.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  System (i) extends the original 2×2 constant-coefficient situation to a 3×3 Jordan block with a triple repeated eigenvalue.  Because the forcing contains terms proportional to e^{2t}—the same exponential that appears in the homogeneous solution—the problem is resonant; simple diagonalisation fails and one must either (a) compute the full matrix exponential of a non-diagonalisable matrix, or (b) execute a step-by-step recursion after factoring out the dominant e^{2t}.  Both routes demand familiarity with nilpotent operators, Jordan normal form and variation-of-constants integrals with mixed polynomial–trigonometric kernels.  None of these issues appear in the original 2-dimensional problem.

2.  Pendulum problem (ii) converts the light-rod point-mass pendulum into a compound pendulum with distributed mass.  The student must compute the composite moment of inertia and the effective gravitational torque, set up the non-linear energy equation, and track the changed limits (60° instead of 90°) which alter the final logarithmic constant from log(1+√2) to log(2+√3).  Although no elliptic functions arise, the presence of two different masses forces additional algebraic manipulation and a deeper conceptual grasp of rigid-body dynamics.

Both enhancements preserve the core ideas (linear ODE methods; energy methods for a pendulum) while introducing higher dimensionality, resonance, non-diagonalisability, coupled integrals, and rigid-body inertia—each an extra layer of sophistication absent from the original kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Take either (i) or (ii).

(i)  A resonant 3-dimensional, non-homogeneous Jordan-type system  
Solve the initial-value problem  
  dx/dt = 2x + y + z + (t+1)e^{2t} + sin t,  
  dy/dt = 2y + z + (t^{2}+t)e^{2t} + 2 cos t,  
  dz/dt = 2z        +               e^{2t} + 3 sin t,  
subject to the data x(0)=1, y(0)=0, z(0)=-1.  
(Observe that the linear part has matrix   
A = 2 1 1; 0 2 1; 0 0 2, i.e. 2I plus a nilpotent matrix with two super-diagonals.)

(ii)  A compound pendulum with distributed mass  
A uniform thin rod AB of length \ell  and mass m is hinged at B.  A point mass \mu  is bolted to the lower end A.  After being perfectly balanced in the upward vertical position the system receives an infinitesimal disturbance.  Prove that the time taken for the rod to pass from the configuration in which it makes an angle of 60^\circ with the upward vertical to the lowest position is  

  T = \sqrt{ \ell ( \mu  + m/3 ) / [ g( \mu  + m/2 ) ] } \cdot  log(2+\sqrt{3}).

We give complete solutions of both parts; the reader may stop after either.

1.  Matrix form

 X' = AX + F(t),  A = 2I + N,    
 N = 0 1 1; 0 0 1; 0 0 0,  N^{3}=0,  

 F(t)=f_1,f_2,f_3^{T},    
 f_1(t)=(t+1)e^{2t}+sin t, f_2(t)=(t^{2}+t)e^{2t}+2 cos t, f_3(t)=e^{2t}+3 sin t.

Although N is not the standard single Jordan block (because of the (1,3)-entry 1), it is still strictly upper-triangular and nilpotent of order 3.

where  

 g_1(t)=t+1+e^{-2t}sin t,  
 g_2(t)=t^{2}+t+2e^{-2t}cos t,  
 g_3(t)=1+3e^{-2t}sin t.

Write U=(u_1,u_2,u_3)^{T}; then  

 u_3' = g_3,  
 u_2' = u_3 + g_2,  
 u_1' = u_2 + u_3 + g_1                  (the extra ``+u_3'' comes from the (1,3)-entry of N).

Initial data: u(0)=X(0)=(1,0,-1).

3.  Integrate from the bottom upward  

(a)  u_3(t)  
 u_3(t)=-1 + \int _{0}^{t}[1+3e^{-2s}sin s]ds  
    = t - 2/5 - (3/5)e^{-2t}(2 sin t + cos t).

(b)  u_2(t)  
 u_2(t)= \int _{0}^{t}[u_3(s)+g_2(s)]ds  
    = t^{3}/3 + t^{2} - (2/5)t + 8/25  
     + (1/25)e^{-2t}(19 sin t - 8 cos t).

(c)  u_1(t)  
 u_1(t)=1 + \int _{0}^{t}[u_2(s)+u_3(s)+g_1(s)]ds  
    = t^{4}/12 + t^{3}/3 + (4/5)t^{2} + (23/25)t + 93/125  
     + e^{-2t}( -51/125 sin t + 32/125 cos t).

4.  Return to x,y,z  
Finally X(t)=e^{2t}U(t):

 z(t)=e^{2t}\Bigl[\,t - 2/5 - (3/5)e^{-2t}(2 sin t + cos t)\Bigr],  

 y(t)=e^{2t}\Bigl[\,t^{3}/3 + t^{2} - (2/5)t + 8/25  
        + (1/25)e^{-2t}(19 sin t - 8 cos t)\Bigr],  

 x(t)=e^{2t}\Bigl[\,t^{4}/12 + t^{3}/3 + (4/5)t^{2} + (23/25)t + 93/125  
        + e^{-2t}( -51/125 sin t + 32/125 cos t)\Bigr].  

Direct substitution shows that these expressions satisfy both the differential equations and the initial conditions.

  
Solution to (ii)

1.  Inertia and effective mass  
Let \theta  be the angle with the upward vertical.  
Moment of inertia about the hinge:

 I = I_rod + \mu \ell ^2 = (1/3)m\ell ^2 + \mu \ell ^2 = \ell ^2( \mu  + m/3 ).

The gravitational torque about B is -M g \ell  sin \theta , where  

 M = \mu  + m/2

combines the point mass \mu  at A and the centre of mass of the rod at \ell /2.

2.  Energy integral  
Release is from rest, so

 \frac{1}{2}I \theta ^{2} = M g \ell  (1-cos \theta ).     (*)

3.  First-order equation for \theta   
Using 1-cos \theta  = 2 sin^2(\theta /2) we obtain  

 \theta  = 2\sqrt{g \ell  M/I} \cdot  sin(\theta /2).     (\dagger )

4.  Time from 60^\circ to the lowest point  
Let \theta _0 = 60^\circ = \pi /3.  Then

 T = \int _{\pi /3}^{\pi } d\theta  / \theta   
   = (1/2)\sqrt{I/(g \ell  M)} \int _{\pi /3}^{\pi } \! csc(\theta /2)\,d\theta .

Put u = \theta /2 (du = d\theta /2); limits u: \pi /6 \to  \pi /2.  Hence

 T = \sqrt{I/(g \ell  M)} \int _{\pi /6}^{\pi /2} csc u \,du  
   = \sqrt{I/(g \ell  M)} [ -ln(csc u + cot u) ]_{\pi /6}^{\pi /2}  
   = \sqrt{I/(g \ell  M)} \cdot  ln(2 + \sqrt{3}).

Finally substitute I and M:

 T = \sqrt{ \ell ( \mu  + m/3 ) / [ g( \mu  + m/2 ) ] } \cdot  ln(2 + \sqrt{3}),

exactly as claimed.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  System (i) extends the original 2×2 constant-coefficient situation to a 3×3 Jordan block with a triple repeated eigenvalue.  Because the forcing contains terms proportional to e^{2t}—the same exponential that appears in the homogeneous solution—the problem is resonant; simple diagonalisation fails and one must either (a) compute the full matrix exponential of a non-diagonalisable matrix, or (b) execute a step-by-step recursion after factoring out the dominant e^{2t}.  Both routes demand familiarity with nilpotent operators, Jordan normal form and variation-of-constants integrals with mixed polynomial–trigonometric kernels.  None of these issues appear in the original 2-dimensional problem.

2.  Pendulum problem (ii) converts the light-rod point-mass pendulum into a compound pendulum with distributed mass.  The student must compute the composite moment of inertia and the effective gravitational torque, set up the non-linear energy equation, and track the changed limits (60° instead of 90°) which alter the final logarithmic constant from log(1+√2) to log(2+√3).  Although no elliptic functions arise, the presence of two different masses forces additional algebraic manipulation and a deeper conceptual grasp of rigid-body dynamics.

Both enhancements preserve the core ideas (linear ODE methods; energy methods for a pendulum) while introducing higher dimensionality, resonance, non-diagonalisability, coupled integrals, and rigid-body inertia—each an extra layer of sophistication absent from the original kernel variant.
